summary: Brazilian Neo-Concretist artist. Known for Bichos (hinged aluminum sculptures meant to be manipulated by viewers) and Walking/Trailings (cutting a Mobius strip lengthwise). Authored the essay 'Nostalgia of the Body.' Minimal quizbowl presence — mostly bonus answers.

works.name: Bichos (1960s)
works.description: Series of hinged aluminum sculptures that can be folded, turned, and oriented in various combinations by viewers. Title means 'critters' in Portuguese.

clues.clue: Hinged aluminum/metal assemblies meant to be manipulated by viewers; title means 'critters'
examples: a set of hinged metal assemblies meant to be manipulated by viewers
a series of hinged aluminum sculptures that can be folded, turned, and oriented in various combinations, called Bichos

works.name: Walking / Trailings (Caminhando)
works.description: Participatory work in which viewers cut a Mobius strip lengthwise repeatedly until it's too thin to cut.

clues.clue: Walking/Trailings: cutting a Mobius strip lengthwise repeatedly until too thin to cut
examples: Lygia Clark's work Walking consists of a paper with this shape that viewers cut lengthwise repeatedly until it is too thin to cut

clues.clue: Brazilian Neo-Concretist artist; wrote 'Nostalgia of the Body' defining Neo-Concretism
examples: In her essay "Nostalgia of the Body," this artist set forth the principles of her Neo-Concretist movement

clues.clue: Neo-Concretism saw art not as 'machine' or 'object' but as 'almost-body'
examples: which saw the work of art not as a "machine" or "object," but rather as an "almost-body"

comprehensive_summary: Lygia Clark was a Brazilian Neo-Concretist artist best known for her Bichos series — hinged aluminum sculptures that viewers can fold, turn, and orient in various combinations. The title means 'critters' in Portuguese. She also created Walking (Caminhando), a participatory work in which viewers cut a Mobius strip lengthwise until too thin to continue. Clark wrote the essay 'Nostalgia of the Body,' which defined Neo-Concretism as treating art not as a 'machine' or 'object' but as an 'almost-body.' She shares the first name Lygia with another Brazilian artist, Lygia Pape, who created Divisor. Clark appears infrequently in quizbowl, mostly as bonus answers.

Artist: this Neo-Concretist's participatory work consists of cutting a Mobius strip lengthwise until it's too thin
